content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

May 5, 2017

Katherine L. Elliott
County Counsel
Mendocino County
501 Low Gap Road, Room 1030
Ukiah, CA 95482

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-17-097

Dear Ms. Elliott:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Mendocino County Supervisor Dan Hamburgh regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act of 1974 (the "Act").¹ Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. Please also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate.

QUESTION

Does the Act prohibit Supervisor Hamburgh from taking part in governmental decisions regarding the County's implementation of Proposition 64, which legalized adult-use marijuana under State law, given that the Supervisor rents residential real property to two of his adult children, and that one of those children's medical marijuana cultivation business has obtained a permit from the Sheriff's Office to cultivate medical marijuana plants at that property pursuant to Chapter 9.31 of the Mendocino County Code?

CONCLUSION

No. The Act does not prohibit the Supervisor from taking part in those decisions so long as those decisions would not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on any of the Supervisor's financial interests.

___________________________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-17-097
Page No. 2

FACTS

You are the Mendocino County Counsel and the authorized representative of Supervisor
Dan Hamburg. The Supervisor owns residential real property within the County that he rents to two
adult non-dependent children. One of his adult-child tenants runs a medical marijuana cultivation
business from the Supervisor's property, and that business has obtained a permit from the Sheriff's
Office to cultivate up to 99 medical marijuana plants at the property pursuant to Chapter 9.31 of the
Mendocino County Code, regulating medical marijuana cultivation within the County.

We have previously advised that the Act prohibited the Supervisor from taking part in
decisions regarding commercial medical marijuana regulation because those decisions would have a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the Supervisor's source-of-income interest in his
adult-child tenant's medical marijuana cultivation business. (Elliott Advice Letter, No. A-16-131.)

On November 8, 2016, Californians approved Proposition 64, the "Control, Regulate, and
Tax Adult Use of Marijuana Act," legalizing adult-use marijuana under State law. Section 3 of
Proposition 64 provides that its purpose is "to establish a comprehensive system to legalize, control
and regulate the cultivation, processing, manufacture, distribution, testing, and sale of nonmedical
marijuana, including marijuana products, for use by adults 21 years and older, and to tax the
commercial growth and retail sale of marijuana."

Among other things, Proposition 64 authorizes an individual to grow up to six plants within
a private residence so long as the area is locked and not visible from a public place; establishes the
Bureau of Marijuana Control within State government and charges the agency with regulating and
licensing the marijuana industry; imposes a cultivation tax on the growth of, and an excise tax on
the sale of, adult-use marijuana; and authorizes local governments to reasonably regulate the
cultivation of adult-use marijuana.

County staff is currently in the process of preparing an ordinance implementing Proposition
64 to be considered by the Board of Supervisors. With Supervisor Hamburg abstaining, the Board
previously directed County staff to draft the ordinance to prohibit a medical marijuana cultivation
business from growing any additional plants at a property beyond the number of medical plants the
business is permitted to grow at that property under Chapter 9.31 of the Mendocino County Code.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits a public official from making, participating in the making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official has a financial interest. Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial
interest" in a decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have material financial effect on one or more of the official's interests identified in
that section. Of the interests identified in Section 87103, the decisions regarding the County's
implementation of Proposition 64 and the facts presented implicate the following:

• An interest in any business entity in which the official has invested $2,000 or more, or in which
the official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of
management. (Section 87103(a) and (d).) Assuming the Supervisor has invested $2,000 or more

File No. A-17-097
Page No. 3

in, or holds a management position in, the Supervisor's residential real property management
business, the Supervisor has an interest in that business.

• An interest in any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment
worth $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b).) Assuming the Supervisor has invested $2,000 or
more in his residential real property that he rents to his adult children, the Supervisor has an
interest in that property.

• An interest in any source of income aggregating $500 or more provided to, received by, or
promised to the official within 12 months of the decision. (Section 87103(c).) Assuming the
Supervisor has received $500 or more from each of his adult-child tenants in the 12 months
preceding the decisions at issue, the Supervisor has a source-of-income interest in those tenants.

• An interest in the official's personal finances or those of an immediate family member. (See
Section 87103.) An official always has an interest in his or her personal finances.

Thus, the Supervisor has interests in his residential real property management business, his
adult-child tenants as sources of income, and his personal finances or those of an immediate family
member.²

Foreseeability and Materiality

Regulation 18701(a) provides that a decision's effect on an official's interest is presumed to
be reasonably foreseeable if the interest is a named party in, or the subject of the decision; and an
interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval,
denial, or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the interest. If
the official is not explicitly involved in the decision, Regulation 18701(b) provides that the
decision's effect on the interest is reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic
possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical.

None of the Supervisor's interests is explicitly involved the decisions regarding the
County's implementation of Proposition 64. Therefore, the effect of those decisions is reasonably
foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or
theoretical.

Business Interest

Regulation 18702.1(b) provides that a decision's effect is material if a prudent person with
sufficient information would find it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision's effect would
contribute to a change in the value of a privately-held business entity.

² Regulation 18702.5 sets forth the materiality standard applicable to a personal financial effect, and
subdivision (c) of that regulation provides that if a decision also affects an official's interest in a business entity or real
property, materiality is determined pursuant to Regulation 18702.1, relating to business interests, or Regulation
18702.2, relating to real property interests, respectively. Because the decisions regarding the County's implementation
of Proposition 64 would affect the Supervisor's interest in his residential real property management business and his
interest in his residential real property, we do not further address his interest in his personal finances or those of
immediate family members.

File No. A-17-097
Page No. 4

The Supervisor's residential real property management business receives rent from his
adult-child tenants. However, the decisions regarding the County's implementation of Proposition
64 will not affect the rent paid by those tenants to the Supervisor's business, and there is no
indication that those decisions would have any other financial effect on Supervisor's business.
Therefore, we find that the effect of the decisions at issue would not contribute to a change in the
value of the Supervisor's privately held residential real property management business.

Real-Property Interest

Regulation 18702.2(a) sets forth the standards for determining whether the reasonably
foreseeable effect of a decision on an official's real-property interest is material. Relevant to your
inquiry and the facts presented, a decision's effect on an official's real-property interest is material
if the decision:

"Would change the character of the parcel of real property by substantially
altering traffic levels or intensity of use, including parking, of property
surrounding the official's real property parcel, the view, privacy, noise levels,
or air quality, including odors, or any other factors that would affect the market
value of the real property parcel in which the official has a financial interest
(Regulation 18702.2(a)(10)); or

"Would cause a reasonably prudent person using due care and consideration
under the circumstances, to believe that the governmental decision was of such
a nature that its reasonably foreseeable effect would influence the market value
of the official's property." (Regulation 18702.2(a)(12).)

The facts presented do not indicate that decisions regarding the implementation of
Proposition 64 would have any effect on the traffic levels or intensity of use of properties
surrounding the Supervisor's property. Because Proposition 64 requires adult-use plants to be kept
within a locked space, not visible by normal unaided vision from the public place (Health and Safety
Code Section 11362.2), the decisions regarding its implementation would not affect the view,
privacy, noise levels, or air quality, including odors, of those properties in a manner that would
affect the value of the Supervisor's property. Therefore, we find that the decisions at issue would
not affect or influence the market value of the Supervisor's residential real property.

Source-of-Income Interest

Regulation 18702.3 sets forth the materiality standards applicable to a decision's reasonably
foreseeable effect on an official's interest in a source of income. Regulation 18702.3(a)(1) provides
that a decision's effect is material if the official knows or has reason to know that the individual has
a business or real-property interest that will be financially affected under the standards set forth in
Regulations 18702.1, applicable to a decision's effect on a business interest, or 18702.2, applicable
to a decision's effect on a real-property interest, respectively.³

³ Regulation 18702.3(a)(1) also provides that a decision's effect is material if it will have a personal financial
effect on the official's source of income under Regulation 18702.5. Because the decisions at issue would affect the
Supervisor's source-of-income interest in his adult-child tenant's medical marijuana cultivation business and the adult-

File No. A-17-097
Page No. 5

Source of Income's Business

As a threshold consideration with respect to the adult-child tenant's medical marijuana cultivation business, we note that Proposition 64 legalized adult-use marijuana within the State, and that the County's decisions at issue merely implement that measure. In other words, the scope of the County's decisions at issue is significantly more limited than the scope of the policy decisions relating to adult-use marijuana determined by Proposition 64. While Proposition 64's legalization of adult-use marijuana will affect the greater marijuana industry within the state, at this early stage in the measure's implementation it is less clear how its state-wide or local implementation will affect any particular medical marijuana business.

As noted above, Regulation 18702.1(b) provides that a decision's effect on a privately-held business entity is material if a prudent person with sufficient information would find it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision's effect would contribute to a change in the business's value.

Because the County's implementation of Proposition 64 would prohibit a medical marijuana cultivation business from growing any additional plants at a property beyond the number of medical plants the business is permitted to grow there under existing law, it would not authorize the Supervisor's adult-child tenant's medical marijuana cultivation business to grow any additional plants at the Supervisor's property. This hard cap with respect to the number of plants an authorized medical marijuana cultivation business is permitted to grow at a property is a strong indication that the decisions at issue will not have a material financial effect on the adult-child tenant's business.

However, it is difficult to assess how particular decisions' regarding the County's implementation of Proposition 64 will affect the adult-child tenant's medical marijuana cultivation business at this early stage in the process because the decisions relate to both adult-use and medical marijuana and involve numerous variables, considerations, and applicable bodies of law. Accordingly, we find that the Act does not prohibit the Supervisor from taking part in decisions regarding the implementation of Proposition 64 so long as those decisions would not contribute to a change in the value of the Supervisor's adult-child tenant's medical marijuana cultivation business.

Source of Income's Leasehold

Regulation 18702.2(b) sets forth the materiality standard applicable to an official's interest in a source of income's real-property interest in a leasehold, and provides that the reasonably foreseeable effect of a decision on the source of income's leasehold is material whenever the decision will: (1) change the termination date of the lease; (2) increase or decrease the potential rental value of the property; (3) increase or decrease the rental value of the property, and the source of income has a right to sublease the property; (4) change the source of income's actual or legally allowable use of the property; or (5) impact the source of income's use and enjoyment of the property.

child tenant's leasehold of the Supervisor's residential real property, we do not further address the adult-child tenant's personal finances or those of immediate family members. (See Regulation 18702.5(c).)

4 In addition, the facts provided refer to decisions implementing Proposition 64, but do not describe a specific decision. This makes it difficult to assess whether the decision would affect the Supervisor's adult-child tenant's medical marijuana cultivation business.

File No. A-17-097
Page No. 6

The Supervisor's adult-child tenants rent his residential real property. The facts presented do not indicate that the decisions at issue would change the termination date of the tenants' lease; increase or decrease the rental value of the potential rental value of the Supervisor's property; change the tenants' actual or legally allowable use of the property, or impact the tenants' use and enjoyment of the property.

Proposition 64 authorizes a person 21 years of age or older to "[p]ossess, plant, cultivate, dry, or process not more than six living marijuana plants and possess the marijuana produced by the plants." (Health and Safety Code Section 11362.1(a)(3).) This authorization does not provide the Supervisor's residential rental property any advantage or disadvantage over comparable rental properties, and there is no indication from the facts presented that the County's implementation of Proposition 64 will have any material financial effect on the adult-child tenants' leasehold that is distinguishable from those decisions' effect on the public generally.

Therefore, we find that the decisions at issue would not have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on the Supervisor's adult-child tenants' real-property interest in the leasehold.

Thus, we conclude that the Act does not prohibit the Supervisor from taking part in decisions regarding the County's implementation of Proposition 64 so long as those decisions would not have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on any of the Supervisor's financial interests.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

[illegible]

By:    Matthew F. Christy
       Counsel, Legal Division

MFC:jgl

sections.question:
Does the Act prohibit Supervisor Hamburg from taking part in governmental decisions 
regarding the County's implementation of Proposition 64, which legalized adult-use marijuana 
under State law, given that the Supervisor rents residential real property to two of his adult children, 
and that one of those children's medical marijuana cultivation business has obtained a permit from 
the Sheriffs Office to cultivate medical marijuana plants at that property pursuant to Chapter 9.31 
of the Mendocino County Code?
sections.conclusion:
No. The Act does not prohibit the Supervisor from taking part in those decisions so long as 
those decisions would not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on any of the 
Supervisor's financial interests.

sections.analysis:
Section 87100 prohibits a public official from making, participating in the making, or 
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the 
official has a financial interest. Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial 
interest" in a decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the 
decision will have material financial effect on one or more of the official's interests identified in 
that section. Of the interests identified in Section 87103, the decisions regarding the County's 
implementation of Proposition 64 and the facts presented implicate the following: 
• 
An interest in any business entity in which the official has invested $2,000 or more, or in which 
the official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of 
management. (Section 87103(a) and (d).) Assuming the Supervisor has invested $2,000 or more  
in, or holds a management position in, the Supervisor's residential real property management 
business, the Supervisor has an interest in that business. 
• 
An interest in any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment 
worth $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b ). ) Assuming the Supervisor has invested $2,000 or 
more in his residential real property that he rents to his adult children, the Supervisor has an 
interest in that property. 
• 
An interest in any source of income aggregating $500 or more provided to, received by, or 
promised to the official within 12 months of the decision. (Section 87103(c).) Assuming the 
Supervisor has received $500 or more from each of his adult-child tenants in the 12 months 
preceding the decisions at issue, the Supervisor has a source-of-income interest in those tenants. 
• 
An interest in the official's personal finances or those of an immediate family member. (See 
Section 87103.) An official always has an interest in his or her personal finances. 
Thus, the Supervisor has interests in his residential real property management business, his 
adult-child tenants as sources of income, and his personal finances or those of an immediate family 
member.2 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
Regulation 18701 ( a) provides that a decision's effect on an official's interest is presumed to 
be reasonably foreseeable if the interest is a named party in, or the subject of the decision; and an 
interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, 
denial, or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the interest. If 
the official is not explicitly involved in the decision, Regulation 18701 (b) provides that the 
decision's effect on the interest is reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic 
possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. 
None of the Supervisor's interests is explicitly involved the decisions regarding the 
County's implementation of Proposition 64. Therefore, the effect of those decisions is reasonably 
foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or 
theoretical. 
Business Interest 
Regulation 18702.1 (b) provides that a decision's effect is material if a prudent person with 
sufficient information would find it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision's effect would 
contribute to a change in the value of a privately-held business entity. 
2 Regulation 18702.5 sets forth the materiality standard applicable to a personal financial effect, and 
subdivision ( c) of that regulation provides that if a decision also affects an official's interest in a business entity or real 
property, materiality is determined pursuant to Regulation 18702.1, relating to business interests, or Regulation 
18702.2, relating to real property interests, respectively. Because the decisions regarding the County's implementation 
of Proposition 64 would affect the Supervisor's interest in his residential real property management business and his 
interest in his residential real property, we do not further address his interest in his personal finances or those of 
immediate family members.  
The Supervisor's residential real property management business receives rent from his 
adult-child tenants. However, the decisions regarding the County's implementation of Proposition 
64 will not affect the rent paid by those tenants to the Supervisor's business, and there is no 
indication that those decisions would have any other financial effect on the Supervisor's business. 
Therefore, we find that the effect of the decisions at issue would not contribute to a change in the 
value of the Supervisor's privately held residential real property management business. 
Real-Property Interest 
Regulation I 8702.2(a) sets forth the standards for determining whether the reasonably 
foreseeable effect of a decision on an official's real-property interest is material. Relevant to your 
inquiry and the facts presented, a decision's effect on an official's real-property interest is material 
if the decision: 
"Would change the character of the parcel of real property by substantially 
altering traffic levels or intensity of use, including parking, of property 
surrounding the official's real property parcel, the view, privacy, noise levels, 
or air quality, including odors, or any other factors that would affect the market 
value of the real property parcel in which the official has a financial interest 
(Regulation l 8702.2(a)(I 0)); or 
"Would cause a reasonably prudent person using due care and consideration 
under the circumstances, to believe that the governmental decision was of such 
a nature that its reasonably foreseeable effect would influence the market value 
of the official's property." (Regulation 18702.2(a)(l2).) 
The facts presented do not indicate that decisions regarding the implementation of 
Proposition 64 would have any effect on the traffic levels or intensity of use of properties 
surrounding the Supervisor's property. Because Proposition 64 requires adult-use plants to be kept 
within a locked space, not visible by normal unaided vision from a public place (Health and Safety 
Code Section 11362.2), the decisions regarding its implementation would not affect the view, 
privacy, noise levels, or air quality, including odors, of those properties in a manner that would 
affect the value of the Supervisor's property. Therefore, we find that the decisions at issue would 
not affect or influence the market value of the Supervisor's residential real property. 
Source-of-Income Interest 
Regulation 18702.3 sets forth the materiality standards applicable to a decision's reasonably 
foreseeable effect on an official's interest in a source of income. Regulation 18702.3(a)(l) provides 
that a decision's effect is material if the official knows or has reason to know that the individual has 
a business or real-property interest that will be financially affected under the standards set forth in 
Regulations 18702.1, applicable to a decision's effect on a business interest, or 18702.2, applicable 
to a decision's effect on a real-property interest, respectively.3 
3 Regulation l 8702.3(a)( 1) also provides that a decision's effect is material if it will have a personal financial 
effect on the official's source of income under Regulation 18702.5. Because the decisions at issue would affect the 
Supervisor's source-of-income interest in his adult-child tenant's medical marijuana cultivation business and the adult-

As a threshold consideration with respect to the adult-child tenant's medical marijuana 
cultivation business, we note that Proposition 64 legalized adult-use marijuana within the State, and

County's decisions at issue is significantly more limited than the scope of the policy decisions

business entity is material if a prudent person with sufficient infonnation would find it is reasonably

Because the County's implementation of Proposition 64 would prohibit a medical marijuana

medical marijuana cultivation business is pennitted to grow at a property is a strong indication that 
the decisions at issue will not have a material financial effect on the adult-child tenant's business. 
However, it is difficult to assess how particular decisions4 regarding the County's 
implementation of Proposition 64 will affect the adult-child tenant's medical marijuana cultivation 
business at this early stage in the process because the decisions relate to both adult-use and medical 
marijuana and involve numerous variables, considerations, and applicable bodies of law. 
Accordingly, we find that the Act does not prohibit the Supervisor from taking part in decisions 
regarding the implementation of Proposition 64 so long as those decisions would not contribute to a 
change in the value of the Supervisor's adult-child tenant's medical marijuana cultivation business. 
Source of Income's Leasehold 
Regulation 18702.2(b) sets forth the materiality standard applicable to an official's interest 
in a source of income's real-property interest in a leasehold, and provides that the reasonably 
foreseeable effect of a decision on the source of income's leasehold is material whenever the 
decision will: (1) change the tennination date of the lease; (2) increase or decrease the potential 
rental value of the property; (3) increase or decrease the rental value of the property, and the source 
of income has a right to sublease the property; (4) change the source of income's actual or legally 
allowable use of the property; or (5) impact the source of income's use and enjoyment of the 
property. 
child tenant's leasehold of the Supervisor's residential real property, we do not further address the adult-child tenant's 
personal finances or those of immediate family members. (See Regulation 18702.S(c).) 
4 In addition, the facts provided refer to decisions implementing Proposition 64, but do not describe a specific 
decision. This makes it difficult to assess whether the decision would affect the Supervisor's adult-child tenant's 
medical marijuana cultivation business.  
The Supervisor's adult-child tenants rent his residential real property. The facts presented do 
not indicate that the decisions at issue would change the tennination date of the tenants' lease; 
increase or decrease the rental value or potential rental value of the Supervisor's property; change 
the tenants' actual or legally allowable use of the property, or impact the tenants' use and 
enjoyment of the property. 
Proposition 64 authorizes a person 21 years of age or older to "[p ]ossess, plant, cultivate, 
harvest, dry, or process not more than six living marijuana plants and possess the marijuana 
produced by the plants." (Health and Safety Code Section 11362. l(a)(3).) This authorization does 
not provide the Supervisor's residential rental property any advantage or disadvantage over 
comparable rental properties, and there is no indication from the facts presented that the County's 
implementation of Proposition 64 will have any material financial effect on the adult-child tenants' 
leasehold that is distinguishable from those decisions' effect on the public generally. 
Therefore, we find that the decisions at issue would not have a reasonably foreseeable 
material financial effect on the Supervisor's adult-child tenants' real-property interest in the 
leasehold. 
Thus, we conclude that the Act does not prohibit the Supervisor from taking part in 
decisions regarding the County's implementation of Proposition 64 so long as those decisions

interests.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does the Act prohibit Supervisor Hamburg from taking part in governmental decisions 
regarding the County's implementation of Proposition 64, which legalized adult-use marijuana 
under State law, given that the Supervisor rents residential real property to two of his adult children, 
and that one of those children's medical marijuana cultivation business has obtained a permit from 
the Sheriffs Office to cultivate medical marijuana plants at that property pursuant to Chapter 9.31 
of the Mendocino County Code?

CONCLUSION: No. The Act does not prohibit the Supervisor from taking part in those decisions so long as 
those decisions would not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on any of the 
Supervisor's financial interests.
